Autonomous UAV Assisted
Volleyball Fault Detection System

P V V L S Tanmay (AP22110020089)  |  Supervisor: Prof. Dr. Rupesh Kumar

Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering  ·  SRM University–AP, Andhra Pradesh  ·  April 2026

2nd Edition of ECE Open House Projects  ·  School of Engineering and Sciences  ·  Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering  ·  Major Project 2022–2026
📋 Abstract

Volleyball officiating demands rapid, precise decisions under occlusion, fatigue, and perspective distortion. This thesis proposes an autonomous fault detection framework integrating UAV imaging with deep learning-based computer vision.

A DJI Mavic 3T drone captures 4K/30fps video from a top-down (nadir) perspective. The system operates as a five-module sequential pipeline:

  • Homography Mapping — 10-point court calibration in <30 sec
  • Detection & Tracking — YOLOv8-Small + StrongSORT + kinematic ball prediction
  • Team Assignment — Automatic centre-line spatial partitioning
  • Serve & Fault Detection — ResNet-34 CNN classifier
  • Centre-Line Violation — Bounding-box trailing-edge logic

Experimental results on real aerial footage achieve mAP@0.5 = 0.830 and fault classification accuracy of 94.12%.

🎯 Introduction

Motivation

Volleyball is governed by the FIVB, where rules are fundamentally geometric. Violations such as serve foot faults, centre-line crossings, and rotation errors depend on precise spatial relationships between players, the ball, and court boundaries.

Problem Statement

Referees must monitor 12 players and a ball moving at >100 km/h simultaneously. Officials miss calls due to:

  • Parallax error and restricted FOV from ground level
  • Multi-player occlusion during dense rallies
  • Fatigue over extended matches
  • Insufficient officiating support in amateur games
Fig. 1: Ground-level referees suffer parallax skew and multi-agent occlusion. The aerial referee provides a global, unobstructed perspective.

Solution

A top-down aerial UAV perspective neutralises occlusion. Unlike Hawk-Eye (fixed stadium cameras), the proposed system is portable and operates on a single drone.

Objectives

  • Design autonomous multi-stage fault detection pipeline
  • Develop homography mapping for metric court coordinates
  • Train YOLOv8-Small on 5,247 domain-specific aerial frames
  • Integrate StrongSORT for stable player identity tracking
  • Implement serve detection and rule-based fault classification
  • Detect centre-line violations using bounding-box geometry

⚙️ Methods & Materials

Hardware Platform

DJI Mavic 3T: 4K/30fps, 4/3 CMOS, 3-axis gimbal (±0.01°), hover accuracy ±0.1m. Altitude: 8–10m nadir. Compute: Intel Core Ultra 5, 16GB RAM, PyTorch 2.1, Ultralytics YOLOv8, OpenCV.

Dataset Construction

31,110 raw frames from 45 min indoor volleyball → stratified sampling + event-triggered extraction → 5,247 annotated frames (31,482 player + 4,100 ball instances). Augmented to ~11,000 training images via Roboflow.

Fig. 2: CourtMapper homography interface — 10-point calibration, RANSAC, canonical court grid (800×400px ≈ 18m×9m).

Module Details

  • Mod 1: 10-pt DLT + RANSAC (ε=3px), EMA smoothing (α=0.05)
  • Mod 2: YOLOv8-Small (11.2M params), 100 epochs AdamW, 960×960
  • Mod 2: StrongSORT (OSNet Re-ID + ECC camera compensation)
  • Mod 3: Centre-line x=412, majority vote (10 frames) for new IDs
  • Mod 4: ResNet-34 serve (τ=0.65) + foot fault (τ=0.5) classifiers
  • Mod 4: Rotation: RF≻CF≻LF, RB≻CB≻LB relative constraints
  • Mod 5: Trailing-edge bbox, T_persist=3 frames, ε=5px
Fig. 3: Ball tracking with kinematic trajectory prediction and player StrongSORT IDs.
Fig. 4: Automatic team assignment — Team A (top) vs Team B (bottom) via centre-line partition.
Fig. 5: Rotation fault tracking — player labels and centre-line (NET_Y=412) visible.
Fig. 6: Serve foot fault detected — ResNet-34 ROI classifier, confidence 87.4%.
Fig. 7: YOLOv8-Small detects all players with StrongSORT track IDs (confidence 0.76–0.87).
📊 Results
0.830
mAP@0.5
0.953
Person AP
0.707
Ball AP
94.1%
Classifier Acc.

Detection Performance

  • mAP@0.5: 0.830 · mAP@0.5:0.95: 0.538
  • Precision: 0.862 · Recall: 0.808
  • Person AP@0.5: 0.953 — strong aerial player detection
  • Ball AP@0.5: 0.707 — reliable despite small size + motion blur

Classification Performance

  • ResNet-34 classifier trained for 150 epochs
  • Training Accuracy: 92.31%
  • Validation Accuracy: 94.12% — good generalisation, no overfitting
  • Foot fault example confidence: 87.4%

Tracking

  • StrongSORT: stable identity tracking across rotations and crossovers
  • Ball kinematic predictor: continuous trajectory under occlusions and high-velocity motion

System Pipeline

Autonomous centre-line init → Player detection → Multi-object tracking → Spatial rule enforcement → Fault logging (JSON) + annotated video output.

💡 Discussion

Key Findings

The nadir UAV perspective effectively eliminates parallax and occlusion issues that ground-level cameras face. Manual homography proves more robust than automated line detection under variable lighting conditions.

Design Trade-offs

Ball detection (AP=0.707) is lower than player detection (AP=0.953) due to small size and motion blur — mitigated by the kinematic predictor. Bounding-box centre-line detection is computationally efficient vs pose estimation (SAM), trading pixel-precision for real-time feasibility.

Comparison with Existing Systems

  • Hawk-Eye: Fixed multi-camera stadium installation — not portable
  • Pixellot: Wide-angle stitching, no rule logic — no fault detection
  • Proposed: Portable, geometry-aware, single UAV platform

Hybrid Design

Data-driven classifiers (ResNet-34) handle perceptual variability in serve and foot fault detection, while deterministic geometric constraints handle rotation validation — ensuring both adaptability and interpretability.

Applicability & Scope

The framework is generalisable to any sport with spatial boundary rules:

  • Basketball — lane and positioning violations
  • Badminton — service fault detection
  • Futsal — offside and boundary monitoring
✅ Conclusions & Future Work

This thesis presents the first portable, autonomous volleyball fault detection system integrating UAV aerial imaging with deep learning and geometric rule enforcement.

Key Contributions

  • 10-point manual homography calibration interface (30 sec setup)
  • Custom YOLOv8-Small on 5,247 domain-specific aerial frames
  • Unified tracking: StrongSORT (players) + kinematic prediction (ball)
  • Automatic team assignment via spatial partitioning
  • ResNet-34 serve & foot fault classifier — 94.12% validation accuracy
  • Lightweight centre-line violation detection via bounding-box geometry

Future Work

  • SAM-based precision foot segmentation for sub-centimetre accuracy
  • Automated homography via deep learning court line segmentation
  • Real-time edge deployment (NVIDIA Jetson AGX + TensorRT, 30fps)
  • Multi-camera fusion for depth and height during net interactions
  • Attack-line violation detection (3m line extension)
  • Generalisation to basketball, badminton, and futsal
